quotes.verbatim_quote:
In all of the tunes here | like to imply more with less. Chords, for instance, are most often only
two or three notes, and the melody is all-important.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This is the author's core aesthetic rule for fingerstyle jazz arrangement, stating that sparse voicings and melodic primacy are the guiding principle throughout these pieces.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The key changes from C to D moving chromatically by half step. Don’t let the chord quality get in
the way of your analysis. It can be simple or sophisticated. It also does not have to have its root in the
bottom position as it does here.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This passage defines a specific modulation technique and gives a rule about analytical flexibility — chord quality and bass position are secondary to recognizing the chromatic movement.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Approaching a chord from a half step above or belowis another accepted way of moving the harmony.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This defines a standard harmonic approach technique (chromatic neighbor chords) as a recognized device for voice-leading between keys.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
A major scale can within itself provide cyclical movement, although not with
exact perfect fifth intervals throughout. This within-the-scale cycle is used here
through the Dm7, after which a’ VI (A’6) of the C scale is inserted. This deceptive
cadence opens up several possibilities
quotes.why_load_bearing: This explains the concept of diatonic cycle-of-fifths movement and the use of a deceptive cadence (bVI substitution) as a modulation tool.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Ultimately, whatever sounds good to the ear will probably work. Just go there. Of course, if all
your playing buddies grimace, you do have to remember that there are community standards of
decency.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This gives the author's overarching rule for harmonic decision-making: ear first, but tempered by shared musical convention.

quotes.verbatim_quote: There are three independent voices at once in this piece, if you count a whole note as a voice. It is. Play the piece with an underlying triplet feel.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This is a direct performance instruction establishing both the three-voice texture and the rhythmic feel required to execute the piece correctly.

quotes.verbatim_quote: This Fats Waller imitation starts with a solid stride style. After a while I sneak bebop into it. Ragtime bebop? Why not?
quotes.why_load_bearing: This labels the stylistic technique of layering bebop vocabulary over a stride foundation, defining the compositional approach of the piece for the player.

quotes.verbatim_quote: I love ballads. They allow a player to have as much elbow room to do whatever he wants, such as playing in double time feel.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This introduces double-time feel as a specific technique available within a ballad context, giving the player a license and method for rhythmic variation.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
This blues is sparse in bass notes yet feels as though nothing is missing. One way this is accom-
plished is by using an almost continual eighth note flow. Joe Pass often used this device.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This defines a specific compensatory technique — sustained eighth-note melodic flow — for maintaining rhythmic momentum when bass notes are sparse, and attributes it to Joe Pass.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
That was a book for pick guitar, and the melodic line was just one note with
no accompaniment. Here | fleshed it out and turned it into a self-accompanied fingerstyle
arrangement. You can do this with virtually any existing tune you have been playing as a single
line only.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This gives a transferable method — converting any single-line melody into a self-accompanied fingerstyle arrangement — making it a generalizable technique, not just a description of one piece.

quotes.verbatim_quote: 3 on 4 takes its name from having many three-beat phrases in the context of 4/4 measures. The melodic line is primarily accompanied by tri-tones. This is the type of sparse playing that could easily fit in a trio or quartet setting.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This defines both the rhythmic concept (polymetric three-beat phrasing over 4/4) and the harmonic technique (tritone accompaniment) that characterize this piece and its applicable performance context.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
A walkdown is a descending bass line with a common tone on top of a chord stack. This is the

device that keeps popping up in this tune along with its hook-like rhythm.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This provides a precise definition of the walkdown technique — a core fingerstyle jazz device — and identifies it as the structural hook of the piece.
